text: The surgeon begins the operation by exposing the surgical field, sweeping the small intestine to the right and towards the pelvis. The cecum is elevated to reveal the tenting of the right colic artery. Dissection occurs around this artery, preparing for transection with a vascular stapler. The right colic artery is ligated at the base, allowing entry to dissect the mesentery from the retroperitoneum. The colon is progressively elevated for a medial to lateral dissection, reaching the hepatic flexure. The duodenum is identified and protected during the procedure. The transverse colon is dissected free from the omentum, proximal to the middle colic artery.

The surgeon rolls the transverse colon and hepatic flexure caudally to facilitate dissection, creating a tunnel under the transverse colon for stapled transection. The hepatic flexure is dissected free, and the right hemicolon is mobilized. Dissection planes are connected from the hepatic flexure to the initial medial to lateral dissection. Lateral attachments of the ascending colon are taken down, and the proximal small intestine is transected near the ileocecal valve. The right hemicolon is elevated, revealing remaining mesentery attachments, which are transected with a vessel sealer, freeing the entire specimen.

The terminal ileum is positioned near the transverse colon in an isoparastaltic manner. Endocyanin green dye is injected, and the da Vinci robot's fluorescence lens is used to visualize vascular perfusion in the anastomotic limbs. Stay sutures are placed at the proximal and distal ends of the planned anastomosis for intestinal manipulation. Enterotomies are created in both the colon and small intestine, followed by a side-by-side stapled anastomosis.

The surgeon inspects the anastomosis, confirming no intraluminal bleeding from the staple line. The common channel enterotomy is over-sewn in two layers: a 3-0 Vicryl suture closes the full thickness mucosal layer in a running fashion, followed by a 2-0 silk suture to imbricate the suture line and close the second serosal layer. Perfusion is briefly rechecked before removing the sutures, completing the operation.
